# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Dockerfile for a Node.js application that needs various system utilities. Install Node.js, build tools, and other dependencies using apt-get. Keep the Dockerfile readable with each install command on its own RUN line. This is for our development and production environments.
# Category: container_security

FROM node:14 AS builder # create a Node.js image that builds your app here 
RUN apt-get update -y && \  
    DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install --no-install-recommends curl software-properties-common -y &&\    
	curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_14.x | bash -  # setup Node.js from nodesourcedelete after this line and comment it out for production environments!    // apt-get install nodejump will not be removed in the final image unless you delete below two lines, but keep them here until then because they should remain uncommented
RUN npm cache clean --force  # make sure we are using a fresh copy of packages.json and package lock file for each run  
WORKDIR /usr/src/app    // set working directory in Dockerfile to where your Node app code is located so you can 'npm install' it from here    
COPY package*.json ./  # use the following command (copy) if there are any additional files or packages that need installing and copying over   Bundle App Source -- copy all source of dependencies into docker image after setting working directory in Dockerfile.	EXPOSE 80      // expose port for development
COPY . .    				// build app & generate dist dir, keep as same location so you can serve the files using npm start or with NODE_ENV=production node ./bin/www    # copy all your source code in this area. You should remove any sensitive information first and then include environment variables 
RUN yarn install --silent     // Install dependencies from package.json by running 'yarn install' on the Nodejs Docker image but using silent mode (--no-progress) which will show build progress if it fails, otherwise just complete with no output at all because of '-s'. Then use NPM_CONFIG_* environment variables set in .env file
EXPOSE 80     // expose port for development and also other ports when needed. For production env you should be using a reverse proxy like PM2 or something similar that takes care about the process management across multiple platforms (Linux, Windows & Mac OS) to manage node processes behind real world server name where your app is deployed  
CMD ["node", "./bin/www"]     // specify default command for container start. When you run docker service without specifying any arguments then it should use this CMD and execute the Nodejs script as a main process running at startup or in daemon mode depending on whether NODE_ENV is set to production
